name: Sindhi
register: Reverent literary contemporary Sindhi in the tradition of the Pakistan Bible Society Sindhi Bible (سنڌي پاڪ ڪتاب) that Sindhi Christians already read and pray in — dignified and gently Arabo-Persianized where the church's ear expects it, yet warm and rooted in native Sindhi kinship words (پيءُ, پُٽ) rather than generic or Islamic-formal substitutes; intelligible to ordinary believers across Sindh.
quote_convention: Double quotation marks "…" read right-to-left; reported and direct speech is commonly introduced with the complementizer ته (ta, 'that'), and the vocative particle اي (ai, 'O') marks address (e.g. اي اسان جا پيءُ).
poetry_convention: Hebrew poetic parallelism (Psalms, Proverbs, prophetic oracles) set as discrete right-aligned verse lines, one clause per line, matched couplets kept adjacent with the second line indented; no forced rhyme or ghazal metre imposed — the parallel sense carries the line.

glossary.note: Filial divine name; پيءُ ('father') is the native Sindhi kinship word the Sindhi Bible uses for God as Father in the Lord's Prayer, not خُدا — آسماني ('heavenly') keeps the reverent height without going generic.

glossary.gloss: our Father (vocative: اي اسان جا پيءُ, 'O our Father')
glossary.note: Primary filial divine name, on the wording of the Sindhi Bible's Lord's Prayer (اي اسان جا آسماني پيءُ). NEVER revert to خُدا or خُداوند; پيءُ is the warm, paternal child-to-father register FHB requires.
Definite filial name; the bare پيءُ carries the definite divine sense in context (cf. پُٽ, 'the Son'), preserving the father–son correlation without an article Sindhi does not use.
Filial; same پيءُ stem as the possessive forms so machine conformance holds; آسماني marks reverent transcendence.

Anchored to the Sindhi Bible (Rom 8:15 / Mk 14:36) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. ابا preserves the Aramaic cry, اي پيءُ ('O Father') the vocative.
REVERSION TOKEN — the traditional generic word for God in Sindhi Christian usage. Non-filial contrast term; use ONLY where the English keeps 'God' as God-as-God, never as a stand-in for پيءُ.

REVERSION TOKEN — the covenant-name rendering of the Sindhi Bible (Yahweh). Non-filial. Note: 'Lord' as a title for Jesus is also خُداوند (e.g. خُداوند يسوع); disambiguate by context. Never a substitute for پيءُ.
The standard Sindhi Bible term (پاڪ = 'holy', روح = 'spirit'); universally recognized among Sindhi Christians. Sindhi prefers this native-formed compound over the Arabic رُوحُ القُدس used in Urdu.

Anchored to the Sindhi Bible (Mt 10:20) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), re-anchoring the Spirit to پيءُ, never changing the construction.
The fixed Sindhi Bible Christological self-title (Arabic-derived); instantly recognized, do not literalize with پُٽ.
The warm, filial correlate of پيءُ, and the native Sindhi word the Sindhi Bible uses for the Son (John 3:16, اڪيلو پُٽ); keeps the father–son bond in the same kinship word-family.
Traditional Christological title. Filial alternative پيءُ جو پُٽ ('the Father's Son') is available and coheres with the FHB vision — native reviewer to confirm which to adopt.
The John 3:16 wording of the Sindhi Bible (پنهنجو اڪيلو پُٽ); اڪيلو conveys 'one and only' with covenant tenderness.
FHB renders 'kingdom of God' filially: بادشاهي is the standard Sindhi Bible word for 'kingdom', re-anchored to پيءُ instead of خُدا.
Standard Sindhi Bible spelling (Christian usage); not the Qur'anic عيسيٰ.
Standard; commonly يسوع مسيح when paired with the name.
Sindhi uses the same word مسيح for both 'Christ' and 'Messiah'; where the English distinguishes, context carries it — reviewer to confirm no separate transliteration is wanted.
Standard Sindhi Bible term; نئون عهد = 'new covenant'.

For Hebrew ḥesed (loyal covenant love). اٽل ('immovable, unshakeable') foregrounds covenant loyalty. وفادار محبت ('faithful love') and the native ٻاجهه ('tender kindness') are close alternatives — reviewer to confirm.

For racham / splanchnizomai (gut-level tenderness). رحم shares the Semitic r-ḥ-m root ('womb') and renders being 'moved with compassion' in the Sindhi Bible; the native ٻاجهه and ترس ('pity') are alternatives, distinct from اٽل محبت above.
The established Sindhi Bible abstract noun (راستباز = 'righteous one'); no substitution needed.
The John 3:16 phrasing of the Sindhi Bible, using the native Sindhi حياتي ('life'); ابدي حياتي ('eternal life') is a more formal synonym if a loftier register is wanted.

glossary.gloss: to be born from a fresh start / anew
glossary.note: The Sindhi Bible wording of John 3:3,7, using the native Sindhi verb ڄمڻ ('to be born'); natural and unmistakable to Sindhi readers, and clear of Hindu rebirth vocabulary.

doctrine_blocklist.why: Means reincarnation/transmigration of souls (Hindu/theosophical); imports rebirth-cycle theology alien to Scripture. Use نئون جنم ('new birth') or the verbal نئين سِري کان ڄمڻ for the born-again sense.

The Hindu avatar — a deity's descent as one of many manifestations; strongly present in the Sindhi cultural context and would distort the unique Incarnation. Use مجسم ٿيڻ ('to become embodied / incarnate').

The Islamic-formal name for God; using it where FHB needs Father imports a non-filial, monadic frame that erases the paternal relationship. Use پيءُ for the Father and خُدا only as the neutral generic reversion token.
